symbols: water jars at center — the six stone jars that will hold the new wine, the old order transformed
grand columned hall — the messianic banquet, the kingdom of God as a feast
musicians playing — the joy of the wedding feast, foretaste of eschatological celebration
green draped canopy — the marriage bower, God's blessing on human union
description: A grand architectural setting with tall Corinthian columns and a canopy of green drapery. Dozens of guests are seated at long white-cloth-covered tables arranged in a U-shape. At center foreground, water jars and vessels stand on the floor near a small table with a candelabra. A servant bends forward pouring from a vessel. Musicians with a large stringed instrument (cello or viol) play at the right. Classical white buildings are visible through the columns against a blue sky. A small dog appears in the lower right.
theological_description: The Wedding at Cana (John 2:1-11) is Christ's first miracle — the 'beginning of signs' through which 'he revealed his glory, and his disciples believed in him' (John 2:11). When the wine runs out, Mary intercedes: 'They have no wine' (John 2:3). Jesus orders the servants to fill six stone water jars and the water becomes the finest wine. This sign operates on multiple theological levels: the transformation of water to wine signifies the old covenant yielding to the new — the water of Jewish purification rites becomes the wine of the messianic age. The abundant, superior wine fulfills the prophetic vision of the messianic banquet: 'On this mountain the Lord Almighty will prepare a feast of rich food for all peoples, a banquet of aged wine' (Isaiah 25:6). The wedding setting itself points to Christ as the Bridegroom of the Church (Ephesians 5:25-27), and the feast anticipates the Marriage Supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9).

scripture_refs.reason: The Wedding at Cana — Christ's first sign, turning water into wine
The messianic banquet of rich food and aged wine — fulfilled in the abundance at Cana
The Marriage Supper of the Lamb — the wedding feast as eschatological foretaste
Christ as Bridegroom of the Church — the wedding context of his first miracle
